1: ḥāmal (H2550) — show compassion/pity; more visceral than rāḥam, often connoting reluctance to destroy. Here YHWH's compassion is ʿal-Yaʿăqōb — on Jacob — resuming the covenant bond despite judgment. bāḥar (H977) — elect/choose; paired with ḥāmal to form the twin poles of election theology: compassion motivates re-election. The re-choosing after exile is not a new covenant but the old one reasserted.
2: gēr (H1616) — sojourner/alien resident. The ironic reversal: nations who were Israel's oppressors now escort (lāqaḥ) them home and become their servants. The word šĕbî (captive) is repurposed — those who held Israel captive are now Israel's captives. This reversal structure pervades the return-from-exile oracles in Isaiah 40-55 and anticipates eschatological restoration.
3: nûaḥ (H5117) — give rest/cause to settle; the same root as Noah (Nōaḥ) and the Sabbath rest. The ʿāḇōdâ qāšâ (harsh servitude) echoes the language of Egyptian bondage in Exodus 1:14, deliberately connecting Babylonian exile to the Egypt paradigm. YHWH's liberation from Babylon is presented as a second Exodus.
4: māšāl (H4912) — proverb, taunt-song, parable; a literary genre that mocks through comparison. The taunt against the king of Babylon is formally introduced as a māšāl, signaling that what follows is satirical polemic, not straightforward historical description. nōgēś — taskmaster/oppressor; the same word used of Egyptian overseers in Exodus 3:7. The repetition is not accidental.
5: maṭṭeh (H4294) — rod/staff, especially of authority; the symbol of ruling power. šēbeṭ (H7626) — scepter/staff; a near synonym but with more specifically royal connotations. The pairing of these two words frames the king's domination as both administrative coercion (maṭṭeh) and royal claim (šēbeṭ), both of which YHWH shatters simultaneously.
6: ḥārâ (H2750) — fury/heat; describes the intensity of the king's blows. biltî ḥāsak — "without restraint/without relief" — the particle biltî negates ḥāśak (to hold back, spare). The Babylonian empire's characteristic is the unrelenting nature of its oppression — no mercy shown, no pause granted. This is the moral charge that justifies the divine judgment of vv.22-23.
7: The earth's response to Babylon's fall: nāpaḥ (H5117, a variant) — to rest/breathe freely; the verb conveys release of pent-up tension. The whole earth šāqaṭ (is quiet, at rest) and pāṣaḥ rinnâ (bursts into song). The passive rest of nature anthropomorphizes creation as a sympathetic audience whose relief mirrors the relief of the oppressed nations.
8: The cypress trees (bĕrôšîm) and cedars of Lebanon (ʾarzê Lĕbānôn) are among the tallest and most valuable trees in the ANE. Their speech — mēʾāz šākabbĕtā, "since you lay down" (ironically echoing the language of sleep/death) — functions as a nature-poem celebrating that the lumberjack who felled them has himself been felled. This is a structural pun: the king who cut down trees is himself cut down.
9: šĕʾôl — Sheol, the underworld; personified here as an actor that stirs itself. rāgaz (H7264) — to tremble/stir up in excitement; the animation of Sheol at the tyrant's arrival is satirical. rĕpāʾîm (H7496) — the shades, the dead; a technical term for the inhabitants of the underworld. This is not the "giants" of Genesis 6 (different context) but the departed rulers of the earth who greet the king ironically.
10: The shades' speech — all of them saying gam-ʾattâ ḥullêtā kāmônû, "you too have become weak like us" — uses ḥālal (H2470, to be weak/sick/wounded) in its niphal form. The taunt is that the mightiest king has been reduced to the same helplessness as the powerless dead. The word ḥullêtā also puns on ḥālal as "profaned/desecrated" — a double shame.
11: gāʾôn (H1347) — pride, arrogance, majesty; the royal self-exaltation. nebel kinnôreykā — the noise of your harps/lyres; the royal music that accompanied the king's feasts in Babylon is silenced. rimmâ (H7415) — maggot/worm; tôlēʿâ — worm/grub. The descent from palace music to bed of maggots is deliberately extreme — the lowest point contrasted with the highest.
12: hêlēl ben-šāḥar — "shining one, son of the dawn/aurora" — the most debated phrase in the chapter. hêlēl (H1966) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible; it derives from hālal (to shine/praise) and refers to the planet Venus as morning star. The Latin Vulgate rendered it Lucifer (light-bearer), a translation that later became a proper name in Christian tradition. The text is using Babylonian royal mythology: the king compared himself to the dawn-star that outshines all other stars. nāpal (H5307) — fallen; gādaʿtā — cut down; the twin verbs describe complete reversal.
13: The five ʾeʾĕleh ("I will ascend/mount") statements: (1) šāmayim (heaven), (2) above kôkĕbê ʾēl (stars of God), (3) at the har môʿēd (mountain of assembly) in the far north, (4) above the bāmôt ʿāb (heights of the clouds), (5) be like ʿelyôn (Most High). The fifth is the culminating claim — not equality with God but likeness to the Most High, the ultimate sacrilege of self-deification.
14: ʿelyôn (H5945) — Most High; the divine title used especially in contexts of universal sovereignty (Gen 14:18-20; Num 24:16; Deut 32:8). The king does not claim to be God but to be like God — the same temptation as Gen 3:5 ("you will be like God"). The trajectory from ambition to Sheol in vv.13-15 forms a rhetorical arc that answers every claim with its opposite.
15: ʾak — "but/yet" — the adversative particle that punctures the five boasts. šĕʾôl — Sheol; yarkĕtê-bôr — the innermost depths of the pit/cistern. The reversal is total: from ascending to heaven to descending to the lowest pit. The word bôr (pit) is used for both cisterns and graves in the Hebrew Bible — the descent is spatial and permanent.
16: šātat (H7582) — to make tremble/cause to shake; the king who once made the earth shake is now himself an object of spectators' scrutiny. hittîr ʾāreṣ — "made the earth tremble" — his former power is now the ironic measure of his humiliation. The verb rāʾâ (gaze/look closely) implies the stunned contemplation of those who see what has become of him.
17: The indictment: nātaṣ (H5422) — to pull down/demolish; lōʾ-pitaḥ — "did not open" — he never released prisoners. The crime is not merely political but against the created order: the king closed what should have been opened (freedom for the captive), creating the disorder that necessitates divine reversal. This frames the oracle within the structure of Isaiah 61:1 (opening prison to captives).
18: kĕbôd (H3519) — glory/honor; all kings lie in glory in their own burial places. The contrast with the Babylonian king's fate is emphatic — every other king gets honorable burial, but not this one. qeber (H6913) — tomb/grave; each king in "his own house" (bêtô), his own dignified resting place.
19: neṣer niʾāṣ — "rejected/abhorred branch" — the most significant inversion in the chapter. neṣer (H5342) occurs in Isa 11:1 as the "shoot/branch" from Jesse's root (the messianic king). Here the Babylonian king is a neṣer but niʾāṣ (abhorred), contrasting directly with the Davidic branch who is honored. The linguistic connection is deliberate: the true king from Jesse's root stands against the false king who claims divine status.
20: zereʿ mĕrēʿîm — "seed of evildoers"; lōʾ-yiqqārēʾ lĕʿôlām — "shall never be named forever." The denial of a name (šēm) is the ultimate punishment in the ANE honor-shame culture — to be nameless is to be as if one never existed. Contrast with Isa 56:5 (those who obey will receive an everlasting name better than sons and daughters).
21: māṣṣēbâ lĕ-ʾābôt — "on account of their fathers' guilt/iniquity." The intergenerational judgment here is not arbitrary — it reflects the ANE understanding that dynasties and lineages perpetuate their founding character. The sons are executed so they do not fill the earth with cities (ʿārîm) — the Babylonian imperial project of building empire through conquest. This is the cancellation of the king's legacy.
22: Four terms for total annihilation: šēm (name), šĕʾār (remnant/survivor), nîn (offspring/posterity), nekeḏ (descendant). The fourfold formulation is a rhetorical device ensuring completeness — no continuation is possible in any dimension of identity. This is YHWH's declaration, not simply Cyrus's conquest; divine speech is indicated by the oracle formula that follows.
23: qipôd (H7090) — hedgehog or owl (species debated); ʾagmê-mayim — pools/stagnant marshes. The imagery of wild creatures and swamp reclaiming a great city reverses the civilizing project — Babylon built on the Euphrates becomes a wasteland. ṭāʾṭēʾ (H2894) — to sweep out; the broom of destruction is a vivid metaphor for total clearance.
24: The oath formula nišbaʿ Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt — "the LORD of hosts has sworn" — invokes the strongest possible divine guarantee. The parallel ʾim-lōʾ clause and the double "exactly as I planned... exactly as I purposed" (ken tihyeh... ken tāqûm) emphasize the inviolability of the divine ʿēṣâ (counsel/purpose). Divine sovereignty is the foundation of prophetic certainty.
25: ʾereṣ — "my own land" — YHWH claims territory: Assyria will be destroyed on YHWH's own ground, the land of Israel. mōʾes — "be crushed/trodden"; harāy — "on my mountains." The defeat of Assyria (fulfilled in 2 Kings 19:35) is presented as YHWH asserting territorial sovereignty. ʿul — yoke; sûbl — burden; the imagery of liberation from oppression.
26: ʿēṣâ (H6098) — counsel/plan/purpose; the term used throughout Isaiah for YHWH's sovereign governance of history (cf. 14:27; 19:17; 23:8-9; 25:1; 28:29; 46:10-11). The plan is kol-hāʾāreṣ — for the whole earth — not merely regional. YHWH's sovereignty over Babylon is simultaneously sovereignty over all nations; no empire is outside his jurisdiction.
27: The rhetorical questions mî yāpēr ("who can annul/frustrate?") and mî yāšîḇ ("who can turn it back?") are the basis for the radical confidence of prophetic proclamation. Isaiah 40:14 and 46:10 develop this theme fully. The extended hand of YHWH (yādô haneṭûyâ) is a standard Exodus image (cf. Deut 4:34; 26:8) applied now to universal judgment.
28: The superscription bišnat môt hammelek ʾāḥāz — "in the year King Ahaz died" (c. 715 BCE) — provides a historical anchor for the oracle against Philistia. The chronological precision is unusual; it may reflect a specific historical occasion when Philistian envoys came to Jerusalem after Ahaz's death to test Hezekiah's loyalty. Isaiah's response would then be this oracle.
29: šōreš nāḥāš — "root of the snake/serpent" (Assyria that beat Philistia); śerāp mĕʿôpēp — "fiery flying serpent" — a more dangerous successor. The escalating threat-imagery (snake → viper → fiery flying serpent) is deliberate: each Assyrian successor is more terrible. This is probably a warning to Philistia not to seek alliance with Judah's enemies, since Judah's "oppressor" will only be replaced by worse.
30: bĕkôrê dallîm — "firstborn of the poor" — an idiom for the poorest among the poor; or alternatively, the "chief poor" (most completely destitute). The verse contrasts the fate of the poor (they will have pasture and safety — lābeṭaḥ) with the fate of Philistia's root (destroyed by famine and remnant killed). The social reversal follows the Isaianic pattern established in 1:19-20 and developed in 25:4-5.
31: ʿāšān miṣṣāpôn — "column of smoke from the north" — Assyrian armies traditionally advanced from the north along the Mediterranean coast. The šāʾ môʿĕdāyw (none straggles/no deserter in his ranks) emphasizes the invader's total discipline and organization — Philistia faces a force without weakness. The gates and cities are addressed in apostrophe.
32: The concluding theological question: what answer to the malʾăkê-gôy (envoys of the nations)? The answer is twofold: (1) Yhwh yissad ṣiyyôn — "YHWH has established Zion" — a perfect tense with certainty; (2) the poor of his people will find refuge in her. This closes the oracle against Philistia by contrasting Philistia's doom with Zion's permanence — the theological crux of chapters 13-23.
